speaker: interviewer
text: Take number two of the interview with Greta Klingsberg, formally Hofmeister. Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do I have to introduce myself?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. It's OK. I just did.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just wanted to add a few things about Laura, who was not only my-- I mean, she was my foster mother, but she had about 30 children under her wing. And it was very hard for us in the beginning, because we were all very artistic in this room. We were room 25 at a house that was called L 410 in-- in Terezin. And she came-- I mean, Laura came and wanted us to be clean and orderly and straighten our beds, and have some kind of normalcy instead of going just to sing and act and write and whatever children would prefer to do. So it was quite a hard time for all of us, but I think it was very important that she put some order and discipline into our life, because it is also a semblance of normalcy that was completely missing there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how old she was at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You know, this is one thing that I can never understand, because everybody asks me this. So I said, look, when I was 12 or 13 years old, anybody five years older is old. I mean, grown up.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was probably a very young woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was probably-- she was a young woman, I guess, and she had-- yes, she was very leftish. She was, of course, Jewish, but very, like, for justice and all this. Of course, there were two main streams in Terezin. It was the Zionists and the communists. But today to say communist is almost like-- you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: A bad word.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's very [INAUDIBLE]. But then, it was like somebody who was idealistic, and anybody who wasn't wasn't like idealistic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was a fascist.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. So its terminology is very bad, but she was a very orderly person. And she knew German, Czech, and Russian, and I guess some English. She was very, very educated, but she was really-- it was one of her main things to be-- that we have to be orderly and clean.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she have her own children there--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --or family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think her husband or her boy-- man friend, or I don't know how you would-- but her partner was hanged by the Germans. So she--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I don't remember. But she was only-- she had a sister also in the Terezin, an elder sister. That's all I know. I don't know anything about her family, because after the war I was still with her a year until I came to Palestine. But later on, we couldn't even correspond then because with an Israeli passport, you are not supposed to go-- I could never see her again because they wouldn't let me in. And later on, even come-- even letters didn't come. It was not safe to write letters from Israel to Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: It'd cause her problems.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] to come in [INAUDIBLE], yes. So it stopped and pittered out, and it was-- it's a very-- great pity.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember at that time your contact with the Germans in Terezin? Was there--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I only-- where once it started, I don't know whether you know this [GERMAN] was this-- it was for propaganda. In the beginning, it was not only for propaganda. We were allowed to make music or deliver a lot of performances, musical performances. And as it was found out that I can sing clean or clear or whatever, I was singing in the [GERMAN] in the choir. And in the [GERMAN], there were also some small parts for a child to sing. And later on, they did the children's opera Brundibar, which was one of the most famous ghetto operas. It was written in-- still in Czechoslovakia. It was there performed in the Jewish orphanage of Prague. It couldn't be performed-- it was by a well-known composer Hans Krasa, who did not survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you meet him? Did you know him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes I did. Of course. But still I can't remember, because he was a grown up person. So I met him. He had to meet me, because I was chosen. I was auditioned. It was a great audition for children. And most of the children were in the choir, but I was the soloist, so I was very, very proud and happy. One of the reasons I also earned my money. I mean, my money was my margarine and sugar. And I was known for years not as Greta, but as Annika, which was the part I did in Czech-- in Terezin in the Opera Brundibar. And this opera, by the way, is now being played all over Germany, Canada, America, England, and by my own translation in Hebrew, by the Ankor Choir of Jerusalem. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a children's choir?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us something about the opera? The story or the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the story in the beginning it was-- yeah. It was-- it's quite nice to say it's a little boy and a little girl, Pepicek and Annika-- myself-- and a cat, a dog, and a sparrow. And children's choir and the children-- and school children. That's the whole cast.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the story is that these little children, Pepicek and Annika, have to find milk for their mother who is sick, and they have no father because he died very very long ago. And they don't have money for the milk-- to buy the milk. So they go on the street, and think, what are we going to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they sing-- all of a sudden, they find an organ grinder who is Brundibar, which is the name of the whole opera. And he's playing there, and people sent to give him money. So they say, oh, that's an idea. Let's start singing. Maybe they'll give us money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they start singing, and, of course, they're being chased away by policemen or by-- also by this organ grinder, because they make a lot of noise and they don't know actually how to sing. They sing-- their voices are too soft and too-- it's too weak. It doesn't make an impression on anybody. So they fall asleep, and they think, what are we going to do? And they fall asleep, and comes in the sparrow, cat, and dog and start to tell them, the only thing you can do, we have to organize it. We have to organize it with all the schoolchildren, and we are going to unite with the all the schoolchildren and we are going to sing the next day. So then we'll be strong enough. So they do it-- and being chased away. And they get money from the grown ups, who are also, of course, children. And the organ grinder takes the money and runs-- and tries to run away, and the children chase him, take the money back again, and everything is OK. They have the money.

speaker: interviewee
text: They are going to buy the milk, and the-- and the moral of the story is that if you unite, nobody can do us anything, not even an organ grinder. Who in the end, is by the way, being persuaded also to sing with the children as not to be revengeful or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they say this story-- the end is like if you unite, and if you stick to the truth and are not afraid, we're going to-- to overcome anything. So of course, the interpretation for the grown ups was completely different. We just enjoyed it, because there was on the-- there was the plot and there was like a school and the cat and the dog and all things that we have not seen. It was the only normal hours of childhood that we could get this way, and it was really a fantastic thing to have.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I sang in it about a 50 times. And the German used it for a propaganda film, which they did for the Red Cross.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that? When--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know exactly, but this you can really find in a history book, I guess. I tell you, I'm really bad on history. I'm just-- so they used it for the propaganda film they called [GERMAN]. The Fuhrer gives the Jews a town.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they spruced up the whole town. They did a lot of-- we had to say, oh, dear. We ate sardines again and all this crap that they told us to say. And they filmed also part of the children's opera, of course. So from there, I have even my picture where I'm on stage in Terezin, and-- anyway, the next day, the-- all the children and all-- what's left of the artists were sent on to east to later on we know by Birkenau and Auschwitz. And most of them perished. Did you-- you say the grown ups understood the message of the opera. Did you have any feeling like that? That it relates to--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was only that if you stick together, this we always had. This we had already before the opera. But I don't also think that-- it's not the message. The message was the interpretation of the grown ups. The message the children's opera has a right to exist by it's own, because it's a very nice opera with music something in between Walt and Stravinsky. Of the time, everybody wrote like this, I guess, and Hans Krasa was also influenced by Schoenberg and all these modern, more modern. But it's a very simple story, and it's one of the few musical things that are not only written for children but performed by children. So that's why I sing, and that's actually what I still do. It has a right to exist on its own, and I'm very happy that it does. And actually now in Germany, even, it is being played in Stuttgart, Frankfurt, and Bonn. I was invited. I didn't go, because it was too cold for me. [LAUGHS] So that's it. But, yeah. The interpretation was, of course, you can imagine. The bad Brundibar yes, who is the bad Brundibar that if you unite against him, you will, of course, gain anything you want to. I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you sing a little part of it? Just a little? Was there like the big finale number?

speaker: interviewee
text: The finales?

speaker: interviewer
text: Or your solo?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the solo-- I can't-- I remember-- I remember-- I have quite a good memory of that time. It is-- yes. [SINGING IN CZECH] and so on and so on. That's Brundibar is defeated. That means-- [CZECH] means Brundibar is defeated. So that was the main scene then. Yes. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] It was very funny to see it in all the different languages later on. So-- but I think it's nice, yeah. That's-- that's the few moments, as I said, of normal life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And outside of that normal life, what was life like there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it became a routine to-- we were-- again, we were also protected, because of the possibility that we were only 30 children together, and we worked together and we learned together. We were not supposed to study--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the work-- What was the work you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know who ever did what, but I--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked outside the ghetto. There was like-- you can call it agriculture, or I don't know what exactly. Anyway, we used to-- yes. And I still love to work in the garden, as you could see in my garden. But it was also a possibility to get some clean air. So probably they did this-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: How many hours a day? Do you remember how--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. Nope. But again, what I do remember is it's cold. It's wet. It's crowded, and it's a lot of sickness around. Still, the hunger didn't so much-- I don't know. You get used to it, I guess. But it's very hard to get used to this cold, and you have no possibility to have more or different clothes. I mean, it's inverse later, but this is-- the crowdedness of it, ever being in the middle of people. That's [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And til when were you together with your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until after this opera performance for the Red Cross or for this movie. It was in beginning of October '45. We went east, what they called east.

speaker: interviewer
text: So let me ask you, before that, though-- when the Red Cross came, was there any feeling that there was a possibility to tell them something that was really true, or was--

speaker: interviewee
text: They saw what they wanted to see, I guess. I-- I cannot vouch for this. I don't have no recollection at all of meeting anybody from the Red Cross. I only know that they were there. But I personally--

speaker: interviewer
text: Had no contact.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Had no-- no-- no memory of that. No. But I know that it was like cleaned out, and we got fresh blouses or whatever. I don't know. It was all spruced up.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think-- What did you think at the time, why they-- what was going on? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Potemkin. Did you ever run of Potemkin? Did it put on a front for something that didn't exist? It was all spruced up, and that's it. That's what I-- if at all remember. I-- I can't vouch for that if it-- I was told it later or if I remember, so I don't want to go into it. It is not truthful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me anything else about the years you spent in Terezin before we--

speaker: interviewee
text: I met a few grandmothers, which I didn't know before, but they all died there. I mean, they were at-- some at the certain age where it was not so tragic anymore. And it was only hard when I came to Palestine later on to tell my father about it. For instance, I should tell my father how his mother died. Did she have Typhus, or-- I don't know. It was-- there was such a lot of sicknesses, and not usual sickness and not like childhood sickness, but like Typhus or-- I don't even know the names anymore. It was a sleepness sickness and all this stuff, and tuberculosis. And it was-- the whole town was always somehow not clean enough. The water was not-- was contaminated, so it was very easy not to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get sick at--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I did, but I got over it so it was not so terrible, I guess. It's OK. No, but I survived it, so I survived Terezin [INAUDIBLE]. But I saw a lot of girlfriends die in there also of typhoid. So it was very hard not to get it, because you were sleeping in such crowded spaces, and there was no-- the sanitary conditions were not of the best. I mean, it's all a matter of relativity, because later on they were non-existent and you still survived. I don't know how. Cold, influenza, all this-- it's very hard today when you say, ooh, it's freezing. And that-- there's no comparison. But you can-- you should never have to go through what you can go through if you have to or want to survive. Or I don't know. But this is great words for today, so you better ask me something. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So what do you remember about leaving Terezin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we all knew in Terezin that to leave Terezin is probably not for the better, so that was definitely-- nobody wanted to go on a transport. Always all the time there were transports leaving, leaving. And you never heard anything any more afterwards. So they said you leave for work. OK. So we leave for work. So my own--

speaker: interviewer
text: But did you-- I mean, were there rumors about where the people were going? Did you have any thoughts at the time what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, I don't have any thoughts from that time, only later rumors [INAUDIBLE], because I was not interested. I had my own world. I had my own music I had my own girlfriends all around me. And we had to work, and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the rumors for the grown ups were probably very [INAUDIBLE]. The transports were all the time, so you didn't want to go into transport. You were-- the only-- my fear was that we were going to be separated. I didn't know what was going on. You and your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: From my sister, from Laura, from anybody that we wanted to-- if we already have to go, we should go together. Which is Laura was separated from her sister, which was all for the best because her sister stayed behind in Terezin. We didn't know what's going to come. I mean, I didn't know. I can only talk for myself. I don't know what the grown ups knew. Meanwhile, I already was 13, 14 years old, so I am like grown up at that time more than now. But-- I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, the transport came, and we had to go. So we had to pack a few things. You packed and you put on the most warmest stuff. We didn't know that anything was like for a few days, and then it was taken away anyway. So who did you go with? You were with your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went with my sister and with Laura. Yes. So we were together until we arrived in Birkenau Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about the trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: The trip? Crowdedness, but still half way no-- I mean, we couldn't get off the train. It was fully loaded. All the sanitary non-existent problems.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have water?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember that time, because I later on went to a few other trains so that is already wiped out, the memory of this one. And especially, we already had still things with us, some food from Terezin, so we were not that hungry. It maybe took a few days. It was only really crowded and quite cold. Always cold, always cold. I never remember that we were warm enough. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And hunger didn't matter so much anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then we arrived at night at some very light-- lit up place. Very noisy and out, out, out. [GERMAN]. And that's what you mostly remember, or you're shown pictures. You don't even-- it gets mixed up. But anyway, you were-- left everything behind in the train, and you were jumping out somewhere into the cold night.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were a few dogs and SS with their big, high boots. And then it went so fast that you did-- we were about 1,200 women, I think. I don't know-- even this, I am not sure. But anyway, there are only 200 selected later on for work, which we also didn't know at the moment. At that time, it was after we are already exhausted from the trip anyway. You were just thrown left or right. Which later on-- again, it is not a memory, it's only a realization-- was one side was for life-- at that time you could still die-- and the other line was it go immediately into the gas chambers, which was my sister's way to go. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- well, luckily we didn't know that as yet, because it's-- again, probably I would have joined her. But it was not impossible. It was such a-- it went so fast, and you didn't anyway know what you do or where you're supposed to do. We're pushed and shoveled and whatever. And again, it was-- it was in October, so it was quite cold. It's Poland, and we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know you were in Poland? Did you know where you were even?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know whether-- I cannot tell you that. I know we went east, so east-- probably somebody told us what it is. They didn't tell us the name also. And in the usual way, now you go into-- being shoveled into some other place and being completely de-nuded. I mean, literary. You had to put of everything, and then you were pushed into another barracks. And then you were-- your head was shaven. Everything, if you had other hair, was also shaved off.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you didn't recognize your next of kin or the next person, because it so completely changed you. And you were-- you were so numb, and-- and you didn't know. So if somebody asks you, how long have you been in Auschwitz? It doesn't matter. It is the first impact, the first three, four days which completely turn you around and mold you into something that you don't even know that existed, or you see things that you never knew could be done to persons, to people, to human beings.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because later on, again, you find some kind-- you find a way to cope with it, or it becomes in a form a routine. But these first days, which is like-- hell has no-- really has no-- there is no image of the [? Dante ?] anybody, or I don't know if there's-- hell exists in our religion. I hope not. That it could be possible what could be done to you. And in a state of [INAUDIBLE] you-- you get numb. You don't even know that you are cold. I mean, you are nothing. You are just nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So slowly you get to recognize your Laura or whoever person you have. There was, of course, no child anymore around. And it was only by sheer chance that I was still-- I mean, I was already almost three years in Terezin, so I was not such a very healthy and good looking girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: The orderly told us, you have to say you are either 18 or not more than 35 and not less than 18 or something, because otherwise you will not survive. And we said, oh, this is just the propaganda. They are so long here, they don't know what they're talking about. We couldn't believe it. It was impossible. It was-- you could not grasp that it is possible I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: That they were murdering people.

speaker: interviewee
text: That they are murdering people on such a scale, and that they are-- what do you mean gas? I mean, who knows about gas? I mean, who would comprehend such a thing? It's very hard to say today, because today everybody knows. And even if you-- if you say doesn't-- didn't exist, you know it did exist, and you know it-- it was possible and it was done to human beings, and it was done to children. It was not done in war. It was done to just normal civil population.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after they-- we were-- all the time we were standing around in nothing, or they didn't even put numbers on us anymore. And they just threw some clothes on us. And I remember I couldn't stand the color purple for a few years, because I got just one little-- you might call it a dress. It was a summer dress of a purple color, and that's all I went through October, November, December. So I-- You had nothing underneath? Did you have any--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. We got some-- we got some shoes, because we had to work. It was not even-- they were-- I think they were wooden clogs or something, but I don't remember exactly. But it was not so comfortable. Anyway, you can imagine, it's like-- Poland is like Russia and it's very cold. But you don't feel it, and you survive with and you don't get influenza. You don't get sick. You cannot afford to get sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all of a sudden, you are almost [INAUDIBLE] before to coldness. Because I know now that I sleep with open window and everything, and everyone says, are you crazy? I says, I don't know. I need air. So it's really unbelievable if you remember it, or if you think about it. After three years of-- even more then, because the war years were all not so. Actually, from '38 to '42, you were not so well-nourished. And then you were thrown out in '44-- in October '44 to this horrible, horrible place. So in Auschwitz, we went through all these things that you had to go through to be counted again and again and again, and you stand out in the open. And one of these days they took 200 of us to throw us into another cattle train or something and back to Germany. Before that, had you done any work there? What was the routine, like you say--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewer
text: --in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no. We did not work. We just were-- no. Deprived of anything and thrown into some barracks. I don't-- I don't remember exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get any food [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think the first day, you don't get food at all. But this really didn't matter. It really didn't-- the whole-- the other things are so much stronger, the impressions. I cannot tell you. Because it was not the problem of hunger any-- with me, personally, so I cannot relate to this if we got food or didn't get food.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few days, a week maybe. I don't remember. I don't remember, because we were chosen to go on to work. And it was already '44 October, so-- so we were thrown again into a cattle-- in a cattle train or whatever. Do you call it a cattle train?

speaker: interviewer
text: Cattle car.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cattle car, something. And-- and-- and to Germany, which was close to Dresden-- something between Dresden and Kremnitz, which was not such a good [INAUDIBLE] on, but it was-- there, there was a-- a-- a munition factory where we were supposed to work. But again, when we arrived, there were already 300, 400 women there mostly from Poland and Hungary. And again, what I remember most is not the huge, tragic big things, because this-- but the small things that they could invent to torment us. It is really-- we were for three, four days on the way-- I don't remember again-- and we were quite where we didn't have any food on the way, on this little way.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came there, and they sat us down and they put a huge bowl of soup or whatever it was, and we couldn't eat. And all those other inmates standing around and implying-- imploring us to eat, because otherwise they will shorten the rations again. But we couldn't. It was impossible.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't explain it. Whether it's the nature of a person that fasts for a few days that he can't immediately start wolfing food. Also he's so hungry that he is so weak that he can't react to this at all in a normal way, or maybe eat three spoonfuls and then that's it. So that was like proof that you don't need so much food, and then they could-- you see, it was all those little things. This laments in your memory that human beings-- that it is possible for human beings to become such callous and such a person. And it was mostly-- it's also-- it was-- it's actually [? where ?] women around that were-- we were under their command. This is my first impression of Oederan, which is the new place, the new concentration camp where we landed now. It was Oederan, and we were thrown into-- I don't know how you call it such. Wooden pallets. There was a little straw on it, and we were two or three together on a very small space. Again, the crowdedness of it, just quite cold, and after a few days we had to start to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in a factory of ammunition, and it was-- I don't know. A little small-- I don't know how to-- how you call this. This is things that I never and I hope never again [LAUGHS] [INAUDIBLE]. Anyway, it was an ammunition factory, and we worked there. And it-- and we went-- and it was a town which was very heavily bombarded maybe because of the factories. Also because it was in the middle of Dresden and Kremnitz, and Dresden was completely destroyed. So we were always locked into this place, and the Germans left for-- for their shelters.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we opened the window and looked around and were happy-- happy the Americans or English or whoever was coming, never thinking that they might also hit us. But you know, this is another kind of self-perseverance, that you don't think that something-- anything can happen to you at that moment. And, well, it didn't, but-- do we have time? So when the front came closer and closer, they started to [INAUDIBLE]. They couldn't kill us anymore. They had no time, and they had no possibilities to kill us in Oederan, so they wanted to take us to some other concentration camps. I don't know which one. Anyway, they didn't-- we didn't reach it anyway, because they put us into these cattle cars again, and now we started to crisscross the place without anything really. I mean, we got maybe some ration for the way, and that's it. And it was-- well, must have been-- when did the war end? In May? So it must have been something like April. Still very cold and wet.

speaker: interviewee
text: And sometimes they let us off these cars on the field to do whatever we had to do, but there was no food around. Maybe we found something in the fields. You know, it was turnips or something. This I remember also that there were turnips around. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] this was very hard, because people went crazy because they were thirsty, for instance. I remember somebody completely crazed-- a crazed woman because of thirst. I think thirst is even worse than hunger. I also remember that the German wanted to give me some bread or something. On the way, we stopped on a stop, and everybody was already out of the car. But Laura and me somehow-- Laura jumped out because she didn't want to influence me.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. We have to stop for a minute. We'll continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. That's why I ask you.
